text: In this video we're going to present a standardized technique for port positioning in laparoscopic colonic resections. After a brief introduction, we are going to discuss port ergonomics and then we are going to proceed in a detailed description of port positioning for right, left and subtotal colectomies. Laparoscopic colorectal surgery offers distinct advantages to patients when compared to open surgery. On the other hand, it is proven to be technically and physically demanding. It often requires full quadrant involvement, has limitations in the manipulation and visualization of tissues, demands long learning curves, and can cause considerable physical discomfort to the surgeon. Inefficient walking postures have proven not only to cause that physical discomfort, but also negatively affect surgical performance. Although minimal access approaches have been described in the literature for nearly all abdominal surgical procedures, there are very few authors that have investigated trocar placement in relation to optimal ergonomics and related technical considerations. This is why port placement and operating room setup have high variability and usually follow individual surgeon preferences.

This is why port ergonomics based on the current literature are going to be discussed next. We are going to introduce concepts as manipulation and azimuth angles, study key elements of postural ergonomy and point out important considerations. Manipulation angle is called the angle between two working ports. It was first introduced by Hanna in his research for optimal port placement when executing a difficult task as intracorporeal endoscopic knotting. He found that a sixty degree angle had a statistically significant shorter execution time and highest performance quality score. A fifteen degree variation was tolerated, but beyond that, a negative effect on the surgical task was observed. Azimuth angle is called the angle between an instrument port and the optical port. Again, Hannah, in his research, found that when these angles are equal or nearly equal, there was a faster execution time on the surgical task at hand, here being intracorporeal knotting. Although unequal angles had a negative impact on execution times, there was no statistically significant difference found on the quality of the knot.

As mentioned earlier in this video, laparoscopic colorectal surgery can be arduous. Often, surgeons have to move their hands and arms in awkward positions and have their heads turned four hours at a time to the direction of the laparoscopic monitors. This can provoke substantial physical strain and subsequently affect performance. Minimal access surgery differs from conventional open surgery in that it uses an image display system as the visual interface between the surgeon and the operating field. Often, the monitor is placed in awkward positions in crowded operating rooms and apart from causing discomfort to the surgeon's cervical spine, can also affect performance, as we will see next. The principle of the motor visual axis stands for the alignment of the visual interface, in this case the monitor, with the hands and instruments of the surgeon performing a laparoscopic task. It has been proven that frontal view direction is superior to side views indifferent if that side view is left or right when a surgical task is performed with statistically significant shorter execution times and quality performance scores. Also, when in frontal view, hand level gaze down viewing resulted again in shorter times and increased performance scores when compared with the eye level frontal view alone.

Next, we would like to point out some important considerations that help prevent risk and increase efficiency. When planning a port placement strategy, it is useful to know that five millimeter ports, when compared with larger ports, have a statistically significant less postoperatory pain score, port-related complications, and patient satisfaction, as far as cosmesis is concerned. Also, ports placed on the upper abdomen, no matter what the size, are significantly more painful than those placed in the lower quadrants. We highly recommend the use of local anesthetic infiltration prior to port insertion, since it has been shown to cause less pain in the immediate postoperatory care. Finally, we recommend insertion using the rapid twist mild push technique, meaning that we rapidly twist the port trocar while only pushing gently, always under direct vision and in a ninety degree angle to the abdominal wall. and no matter what the pathology, we always use a wound protector in our extraction sites.

The surgeon describes the port setup for ileocecal resections, inserting a ten millimeter port in the inferior part of the umbilicus and two five millimeter operating ports: one two fingerbreadths medial to the superior anterior iliac spine in the left iliac fossa, and the other just above the pubis. For right hemicolectomies, a ten millimeter port is placed two fingerbreadths medially to the left superior anterior iliac spine for clipping mesocolic vessels, with two five millimeter ports placed suprapubically and on the epigastrium just left of the midline for dissection and exposure. Extraction is performed through a midline or right flank incision.

The surgeon begins port placement for left hemicolectomies and anterior resections by inserting a ten millimeter optical port at the umbilicus. A twelve millimeter port is positioned two fingerbreadths medially and superiorly to the anterior iliac spine to avoid the pelvic brim during dissection of the lateral right part of the high rectum. A five millimeter operating port is placed in the right upper quadrant, ensuring a minimum distance of one adult fist from the twelve millimeter port to prevent instrument conflict during pelvic dissection. An assistant five millimeter port is placed in the left flank, with specimen extraction through a midline or left flank incision using a wound protector.

The surgeon describes two setups for septotal colectomies. In the first setup, four 5mm ports are placed: one two finger breadths medially and superiorly to the right superior anterior iliac spine, another in the right upper quadrant, one in the left iliac fossa, and one in the subxiphoid area. A 12mm port is placed suprapubically for stapling and vascular clips. In the second setup, a 12mm port is placed in the right iliac fossa, a 10mm port in the left iliac fossa, and three 5mm ports in previously described positions. A funnestill incision is used for extraction.

In this video, we try to provide a strategy guide for port placement as far as different kinds of laparoscopic colectomies are concerned, based on the current literature. Our goal is to facilitate the surgeon improve laparoscopic performance and avoid unnecessary fatigue for the surgical team. Thank you.
